data.title: What’s next for Apple after Tim Cook’s CEO transition announcement?
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) announced a major leadership transition on April 21, with Tim Cook stepping down as CEO after a 15-year tenure that saw the company’s stock appreciate more than 10x. John Ternus, currently head of hardware engineering, will assume the CEO role on Sept. 1, while Cook transitions to the position of executive chairman.

Despite the historic nature of this succession, analyst sentiment remains cautiously mixed, with experts weighing the promise of hardware-driven innovation against concerns about elevated valuations and AI strategy execution.

What Do Seeking Alpha Analysts Say About Apple’s Future?

Optimists pointed to Apple’s differentiated AI strategy, which emphasizes on-device inference and privacy-centric edge computing rather than the heavy capital expenditure approach taken by hyperscalers. With 2.5 billion active devices, Apple’s installed base provides a massive platform for localized AI integration without the depreciation costs associated with large-scale data center investments. John Ternus’s deep hardware engineering background is considered a potential catalyst for accelerating product innovation in the AI era.

Pessimists, however, highlighted Apple’s elevated valuation of 32x to 34x forward earnings, which trades at an 18.7% premium to Big Tech peers. Critics pointed to the company’s perceived lack of AI leadership compared to other Magnificent 7 companies, as well as operational risks including rising memory costs, TSMC capacity constraints, and intensifying competition in China.

Here’s a breakdown of what some analysts had to say:

  • JR Research, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Buy: “I see Apple doubling down on hardware and product development to navigate this AI era, while integrating deeper with third party models… Tim Cook will assume the executive chair, helping to maintain its core strategic focus and organizational culture.” - Apple: Tim Cook Hands Over, But This Is Not A Great Reset

  • The Value Portfolio, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Buy: “AAPL’s strategy centers on margin improvements, aggressive share repurchases, and a focused business model, driving strong EPS and net income growth… Recent results show double-digit sales and nearly 20% YoY net income growth, with robust performance across categories and geographies.” - Apple’s New Leadership Preserves The Story

  • Danil Sereda, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Hold: “AAPL’s AI strategy emphasizes on-device inference and privacy-centric edge computing, avoiding heavy CAPEX and leveraging its 2.5 billion active device base… Despite stable EPS growth and fair valuation, I maintain a ‘Hold’ rating, awaiting clearer upside catalysts and post-transition consolidation.” - Apple: Tim Cook Stepping Down As CEO Opens A New Chapter

  • Deep Value Investing, Analyst, Rating: Hold: “The bull case hinges on disciplined AI. The fact that Apple is behind the other Magnificent 7 in the AI race is actually a plus… The company needs to monetize default Siri placement for outside LLM providers instead of funding in-house models or building or renting out data centers.” - Apple: Next CEO Should Rent Out Siri, Not Rebuild It

  • YR Research, Analyst, Rating: Hold: “I upgrade AAPL to ‘Hold’—valuation already reflects its moat and near-term growth, but long-term upside is limited without a new growth catalyst… While Cook’s time at Apple cannot be described as anything less than extraordinary, some investors grew increasingly frustrated.” - Apple: Tim Cook Stepping Down On A High Note (Rating Upgrade)

  • Bill Maurer, Analyst, Rating: Hold: “AAPL maintains strong fundamentals with robust iPhone sales, expected 15% Q2 revenue growth, and significant capital returns… Valuation remains elevated at 31.75x 2026 EPS, trading at an 18.7% premium to Big Tech peers, potentially limiting near-term AAPL upside.” - Apple Enters New Leadership Era

  • Louis Gerard, Analyst, Rating: Sell: “Leadership transition to John Ternus signals a shift toward engineering and product innovation but introduces the risk of multiple compression if AI strategy lags… Apple Inc. is rated a Sell due to its elevated 34x earnings multiple amid strategic uncertainty and AI execution concerns.” - Apple: The CEO Transition Is A Multiple Compression Catalyst

What Do the Quant Ratings Say?

Apple currently carries a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha’s quant system. While the company demonstrates exceptional profitability, its premium valuation and mixed growth outlook present challenges for investors seeking outperformance.

Profitability (A+): Apple continues to demonstrate strong profitability metrics, earning an A-range grade. The company delivered nearly 20% year-over-year net income growth with robust performance across product categories and geographies.

Valuation ((F)): Valuation scores in the D/F range, reflecting the stock’s 32x to 34x forward P/E multiple. AAPL trades at a 10% premium above its five-year average and the highest multiple among Big Tech peers, suggesting limited room for further expansion.

Growth ((D)): Growth metrics present a mixed picture. Consensus projects a 5-year EPS CAGR of 13.44%, which supports the current valuation but may not deliver market-beating returns at the current multiple.

Momentum ((B)): Momentum remains stable, with shares trading near a fair value estimate of $281.1 based on a 30x multiple, representing approximately 3% to 4% upside from current levels.

Earnings Revisions (B+): Q2 guidance calls for 13% to 16% revenue growth with strong margins, providing near-term support. However, tougher comparisons loom for Q3, which analysts view as a more defining quarter for the stock.

What’s the Latest News on Apple?

Apple’s transition comes amid competitive pressure from peers including Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), and Alphabet (GOOG). The company guided for approximately 15% Q2 revenue growth, though some investors have grown frustrated with Apple’s conservative approach, including the shuttered Apple Car initiative and what some perceive as a lack of AI leadership.

Operational risks remain a concern for the incoming CEO. Analysts flagged potential TSMC (TSM) N3 capacity constraints and rising memory prices as challenges that could pressure margins in upcoming quarters. Competition in China, Apple’s most important ex-U.S. market, also presents ongoing uncertainty, though recent Q1 performance in the region showed strength.

data.title: Texas Instruments sees bullish views at GF Securities after Q1 results
data.description:
GF Securities (Hong Kong) raised the price target on Texas Instruments' (TXN) stock to $270 from $220 while maintaining its Buy rating after first quarter results beat estimates.

Analysts led by Alicia Xia said that Industrial recovery strengthened, while Automotive stabilized. 

"At the end-market level, the recovery continues to broaden, with strength extending across a wider set of end markets. Industrial grew more than 30% YoY and over 20% QoQ, with the mgmt. highlighting that demand strength has broadened beyond a few resilient verticals to include the long tail of customers," said Xia and her team.

The analysts added that Automotive was up mid-single digits year-over-year and roughly flat quarter-over-quarter, with China weaker while other regions improved, indicating a more uneven recovery profile. Communications Equipment rebounded strongly, while Personal Electronics remained relatively stable, the analysts noted. 

"Overall, industrial appears to be entering a more sustained recovery phase, while automotiveis stabilizing at elevated levels rather than accelerating," the analysts added.

In addition, Xia and her team said that Data Center remained the primary structural growth driver.

Data center revenue grew about 90% year-over-year and over 25% quarter-over-quarter in the first quarter of 2026, marking continued momentum driven by AI-related power demand, the analysts noted.

The analysts said that the management expects Industrial and Data Center to lead growth again in the second quarter, although they remain cautious on the second half given prior false starts in the broader cycle.

Inventory and cash flow trends improved, with inventory levels declining  quarter-over-quarter and capex guidance remaining below recent peaks, supporting a recovery in free cash flow, according to the analysts.

"Management also emphasized that pricing was stable both QoQ and YoY, which is notable given that 1Q26 pricing is usually modestly down. Overall, the mgmt. reinforced a combinationof cyclical recovery in core markets and an increasing contribution from AI-driven demand," said Xia and her team.

data.title: ServiceNow's hybrid pricing gains ground as 50% of new revenue is non-seat-based: CFO
data.description:
ServiceNow's (NOW) hybrid pricing shift has led to 50% of all new business coming from non-seat-based pricing models, according to Chief Financial Officer and President Gina Mastantuono during an interview with Seeking Alpha. 

This is important because a primary reason behind the recent decline of enterprise software stocks stems from concerns over seat-based pricing models. The idea is that as enterprises increase their usage of artificial intelligence tools, efficiencies will improve and headcounts will be reduced or remain flat, thus reducing the number of available seats at an enterprise.

However, despite the rise of AI agents, ServiceNow has not been losing seats.

"AI is unlocking productivity, but seat count remains healthy and continued to grow year over year in Q1," Mastantuono said. "We have a hybrid model that is seat-based first, and as you consume more tokens, fees go in. Over 50% of our new business comes from non-seat-based models. It's not new. We've had this for years, based on infrastructure and services. What's new is the AI consumption model on top of that."

First-quarter results featured 22.1% year-over-year revenue growth and a 2026 outlook that surpassed market expectations. The company's AI ACV is now on track to surpass $1.5B this year, which is a 50% jump from the prior target of $1B. 

ServiceNow shares took a beating after the release. The stock made a historic 19% plummet on Thursday, April 23. Financial analysts linked the drop to a slight decline in the gross margin outlook related to the recent acquisition of Armis. Furthermore, the ongoing conflict in the Middle East created a 75 basis point headwind due to delayed closings of several large, on-premise deals in the region.

"We're looking at short-term margin headwinds here (full-year non-GAAP operating margin to 31.5% from the prior 32%, and full-year free cash flow margin to 35% from 36%)," said Seeking Alpha analyst Oliver Rodzianko. "That's the basic math behind the sell-off, but this is incredibly short-term and misses the fact that the Armis acquisition will only dilute profitability temporarily."

Most investment firms remained upbeat on the stock. BTIG retained its Buy rating but lowered its price target to $150 from $185. Needham also maintained its Buy rating and reduced its price target to $115 from $155. Finally, Stifel reiterated its Buy rating, lowering its target to $120 from $135.

"Zooming out, we expect NOW's numbers to increase as the year progresses," said Stifel analysts Allan Verkhovski and Nick Dannewitz in an investor note. "The company has successfully closed its pending acquisitions, positioning itself to deliver a comprehensive platform with AI, data, security, and governance embedded into every product and package. We look forward to Financial Analyst Day on May 4th to learn more about how this integrated approach can further drive a flywheel of AI consumption on a multi-year basis."

Despite the noise that large language models have the potential to upend traditional enterprise software firms, Mastantuono said AI has been a driving force behind ServiceNow's sustained 20%-plus revenue growth quarter after quarter.

"Our product portfolio continues to outperform," she said. "The number of customers spending on our AI control tower continues to increase. The signal is clear: customers are clearly moving from experimentation to scale … AI structurally increases demand. It does not decrease it. And we don't just sell this platform. We run on it."

data.title: New York City is set to make history with its first full casino opening
data.description:
Resorts World plans to open a full casino in New York City next week pending final approval from the New York Gaming Commission. The opening will mark the first time that New York City has live casino table games.

After landing its license, Resorts World New York City spent $5.5B to expand a large video lottery terminal casino at Aqueduct Racetrack in Ozone Park into a full-scale casino. Resorts World New York City is operated by Malaysia-based Genting Group, a global company founded in 1965, with destination resort operations in the Catskills, Hudson Valley, Las Vegas, Bimini, Malaysia, Singapore, and the United Kingdom.

Resorts World's Queens casino is not the only full-scale casino coming to New York City soon. In December, the New York State Gaming Commission approved three locations, including a Bally's (BALY) location on land next to the former Trump Golf Links at Ferry Point in the Bronx and a casino and entertainment center near Citi Field, backed by Mets owner Steve Cohen.

For the broader New York gambling market, the opening means that there is one fewer reason to trek to Atlantic City casinos operated by MGM Resorts (MGM) or Caesars Entertainment or the Mohegan Sun and Foxwoods in Connecticut. The new casino is positioned to capture not just casual local gamblers but also the high-rollers and international visitors who pass through New York City in enormous numbers and have previously had nowhere to play within city limits..Looking ahead, two other operators were awarded downstate casino licenses by the New York State Gaming Commission. Hard Rock International, backed by New York Mets owner Steve Cohen, plans to build the Metropolitan Park casino resort adjacent to Citi Field in Queens, while Bally's Corporation (BALY) won the license for a site at Ferry Point near the Whitestone Bridge in the Bronx, on land previously occupied by a Trump Organization golf course. Both are ground-up construction projects, with anticipated opening timelines pushed out to 2030, which gives Resorts World New York City a multi-year window as the only full casino game in town.

data.title: What’s next for UnitedHealth Group after Q1 earnings beat and guidance raise
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group (UNH) delivered a strong first quarter as shares surged 7% following better-than-expected results. 

Still, despite the recovery evidence, analyst sentiment leans bullish as experts weigh the company’s turnaround progress against persistent structural risks in the healthcare sector.

What Do Seeking Alpha Analysts Say About UnitedHealth’s Future?

Optimists pointed to the company’s margin expansion driven by premium repricing, AI-driven cost savings, and plan restructuring. The improved Medical Care Ratio of 83.9%, which came in better than the expected 85.5%, represented a 90-basis-point improvement from the prior year period. Management’s confidence was reflected in the raised 2026 guidance, with the company also committing $1.5B in AI investments to streamline administrative workflows, claims processing, and member inquiries.

Skeptics, however, highlighted the risks of persistent elevated Medical Loss Ratio and government reimbursement volatility. The company lost approximately 1M Medicare Advantage members as it strategically exited unprofitable counties, and execution risks remain where aggressive premium hikes could drive policyholders to competitors. While the bleeding has stopped, the MLR is still elevated and must be addressed over time.

Here’s a breakdown of what some analysts had to say:

  • Quad 7 Capital, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Buy: “This is an exciting result. And it was not a mild beat either. ... We believed that 2026 would see an inflection lower on the ratio, and we are getting those results now.” - UnitedHealth Is Back In A Big Way

  • Jonathan Weber, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Buy: “UNH's margin expansion in UnitedHealthcare offset weaker Optum results, marking a reversal of prior cost headwinds and supporting EPS growth.” - UnitedHealth Group: Soaring On Big Profit Beat

  • The Asian Investor, Analyst, Rating: Strong Buy: “The insurance platform actually benefited from a lower Medical Care Ratio… the utilization of AI and higher premiums could drive a fundamental recovery for UnitedHealth Group’s shares in 2026.” - UnitedHealth Group: A 2026 Turnaround Story

  • DOCSHAH FINANCIAL, Analyst, Rating: Strong Buy: “For investors who are comfortable with the risk profile and have a 2-3 year time horizon, I believe UnitedHealth Group offers a compelling risk/return profile with the potential to return 54% from today’s current level.” — UnitedHealth Group: Margin Expansion Will Drive Stock Price Higher

What Do the Quant Ratings Say?

UnitedHealth currently carries a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha’s quant system with a score of 3.46. While UNH is the industry bellwether with a market cap of $321 billion, it currently ranks behind smaller peers like Centene (CNC) in the managed healthcare sector.

Profitability: The company demonstrates strong profitability performance, supported by robust operating income of $9.0 billion in Q1, a massive sequential improvement from the $0.4 billion in Q4. UnitedHealthcare expanded operating margins by 40 basis points to 6.6% compared to 6.2% in the first quarter of 2025.

Valuation: UNH trades at a forward P/E of 19.35, receiving a moderate grade as it remains 28% below its historical 3-year average P/E ratio of 23.9X. If the company revalues to historical levels, shares could have fair value in the neighborhood of $480 per share.

Growth: Growth metrics present concerns, with year-over-year revenue growth at just 2%. However, management expects at least $440 billion in revenue for 2026 and projects 13-16% long-term earnings growth.

Momentum: The stock receives a Hold-level grade as it recovers from previous lows around $230 to $240 but still faces sector-wide pressure. Shares are up approximately 43% since August 2025.

Earnings Revisions: The positive trend in revisions following the guidance raise to greater than $18.25 per share compares favorably to the previous consensus of $17.87, with management signaling improved confidence in the turnaround trajectory.

What’s the Latest News on UnitedHealth?

UnitedHealth reported Q1 revenue of $111.7B, representing 2% year-over-year growth and beating estimates by $2.06B, while adjusted EPS of $7.23 crushed expectations by $0.63. Management also raised its full-year 2026 adjusted EPS outlook to greater than $18.25, up from the previous guidance of more than $17.75..

With the financial results, UnitedHealth announced plans to repurchase at least $2B of its common stock by the end of Q2 2026. The company also completed the sale of the Optum UK business, with $400M in net proceeds committed to the United Health Foundation.

The strong results lifted managed care peers across the board, with Humana (HUM), CVS Health (CVS), Cigna (CI), Clover Health (CLOV), and Alignment Healthcare (ALHC) all trading higher following UNH’s announcement. The company has earmarked $1.5B in capital for its AI-first investment campaign in 2026, targeting both Optum and UnitedHealthcare subsidiaries to improve performance and support future innovations.

data.title: Strait of Hormuz likely will not fully reopen for months, Baker Hughes executive says
data.description:
Oilfield services company Baker Hughes (BKR) assumes in its financial guidance that the U.S.-Iran war continues at least through the end of June, and the Strait of Hormuz may not be fully operational until later this year, CFO Ahmed Moghal said Friday.

"There's still a great deal of uncertainty regarding ultimately the duration and depth of the conflict," Moghal said on the company's Q1 earnings conference call.

The assumption that Hormuz may not reopen for months is widely shared in the energy industry, as the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas released a survey this week of nearly 100 oil and gas executives that showed nearly 80% believe the strait will not reopen until August or later.

"Geopolitical risk has become a structural reality for oil and gas markets," likely resulting in "persistent risk premiums for oil and LNG prices," Baker Hughes (BKR) CEO Lorenzo Simonelli said on the call.

Tanker traffic through Hormuz remains very low as the conflict enters its eighth week, as the U.S. and Iran have both seized commercial ships as they try to enforce competing blockades in and around the strait.

Crude oil futures rose sharply for the week on disappointment that the Strait of Hormuz has not yet reopened but turned mixed Friday as U.S. and Iranian officials head to Pakistan for more talks after planned meetings were postponed earlier in the week.

U.S. negotiators Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are traveling to Islamabad in an effort to renew talks with Iran, while Iran Foreign Minister Araghchi will present a new written response to a U.S. proposal for a peace deal while in Pakistan, the New York Times reported.

Recent developments appear to indicate that "traders are getting increasingly comfortable with the idea that the kinetic phase of the U.S.-Iran conflict is ending, or has already ended, and that an economic war is becoming entrenched," Macquarie Global FX & Rates Strategist Thierry Wizman wrote.

"Even a full reopening may still leave flows taking several months to normalize, creating additional tightness, especially in diesel and jet fuel, and forcing countries and companies to curb demand," Saxo Bank's Ole Hansen said in a note.

Front-month Nymex crude for June delivery fell 1.5% to $94.40/bbl on Friday, but front-month Brent June crude (CO1:COM) gained 0.2% to $105.33/bbl, and front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for May delivery slid 3.5% to $2.523/MMBtu.

For the full week, Nymex crude and Brent crude jumped 14.3% and 16.5%, respectively, while Nymex natural gas fell 5.6%.

Energy stocks, as indicated by the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE), rose 3.3% this week.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Air Force, Navy plan to boost purchases of Lockheed F-35 jets over five years
data.description:
The U.S. Air Force and Navy proposed stepping up purchases of Lockheed Martin's (LMT) F-35 fighter jet over the next five years, a show of confidence for a plane that critics have said is becoming obsolete, Bloomberg reported Friday.

Planned purchases of the Air Force model would rise steadily from 38 jets in FY 2027 to 42 in 2028, 46 in 2029, and 48 in both 2030 and 2031, while purchases by the Navy and Marine Corps would jump to 47 jets next year from this year’s 23, before dropping to 43 in 2028 and 38 or fewer each year during 2029-31, according to new projections.

The increased quantities are part of the Trump administration's request to Congress to increase the defense budget by more than 40% to $1.5T, which is bound to meet resistance from both Democrats and fiscal conservatives.

The planned purchases would be good news for Lockheed (LMT), with the total number of F-35s requested up to 85 jets from the 47 Congress approved for this year.

The projections failed to improve the performance of Lockheed's (LMT) stock, which has declined for nine straight sessions—slumping 17.1% during the period—and fell another 3.1% on Friday to finish at $513.45, its lowest close since January 7.

data.title: First Business targets 10% annual loan and core deposit growth for 2026 as CEO transition nears
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Business Financial Services (FBIZ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Corey Chambas said the company delivered “a strong start to 2026,” adding that it “won new relationships in a highly competitive environment, growing loans and deposits at a pace that well exceeded our expectations,” and that fee income grew “nearly 16% year-over-year” with Private Wealth posting “record revenues.” (CEO & Director Corey Chambas)
  • Chambas framed the quarter around profitability and capital, stating, “we grew net income and earnings per share by more than 9% over last year's first quarter,” and “our strong earnings and disciplined capital deployment drove 14% year-over-year growth in tangible book value per share.” (CEO & Director Corey Chambas)
  • President David Seiler emphasized the growth mix and timing: “In the first quarter, we grew loans by $126 million or 15%,” with “$90 million or 72% in March,” and said pipelines are “lighter going into Q2” with “some known payoffs,” leading to an expectation that “the second quarter [will] be lighter on growth than Q1” while remaining “on track to achieve our 10% annual growth goal for 2026.” (President & COO David Seiler)
  • Seiler highlighted deposits and fee income, saying “first quarter balances were up 18% from the linked quarter and up 14% year-over-year,” and Private Wealth produced “record revenue of $3.9 million, up 11% year-over-year,” representing “more than 40% of our total quarterly fee income.” (President & COO Seiler)
  • CFO Brian Spielmann said margin results reflected quarter mechanics and growth timing: “First quarter net interest margin increased 3 basis points to 3.56%,” and “excluding” fewer accrual days, “first quarter NIM was 3.61%,” while the adjusted Q4 comparison would have been “3.63%.” (CFO & Treasurer Brian Spielmann)
  • Seiler disclosed a leadership change and performance context: “this is Corey's last call before his retirement next week,” and added the company will be “very happy that Corey will be continuing to serve on our Board.” (President & COO Seiler)

Outlook

  • Spielmann guided to the annual net interest margin framework: “we think the early momentum of C&I loan growth in Q1 positions us well to operate within or toward the lower to middle portion of our targeted 3.60% to 3.65% range for the year,” assuming “a stable to modestly changing interest rate environment.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • Management reiterated growth and fee objectives: Seiler said the company targets “10% loan and core deposit growth on an annual basis,” while Spielmann stated, “Our strong first quarter fee income supports our expectation of 10% growth for the full year compared to 2025.” (President & COO Seiler; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • Seiler flagged near-term moderation after Q1 pull-forward: “After an extremely strong first quarter, our pipelines are lighter going into Q2,” and “we will have some known payoffs in the second quarter,” with “normalization in the second half of the year.” (President & COO Seiler)
  • No management EPS or revenue guidance was provided in the prepared remarks or Q&A in this transcript.

Financial Results

  • Spielmann reported net interest margin details and drivers, including “3.56%” reported NIM and “3.61%” excluding fewer accrual days, and said the adjusted change “primarily reflects the late quarter timing of loan growth.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • On fees and expense cadence, Spielmann stated Q4 comparisons were affected by “an accounting classification change,” and reiterated Q1 fee strength as a “good starting point for quarterly fee income in 2026,” while acknowledging compensation seasonality: “Compensation expense increased by about $1.4 million” from Q4, and FTEs were “up about 5.7% from a year ago.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • On taxes and operating leverage, Spielmann said, “The effective tax rate was 15.2% for the first quarter,” and the company expects “16% to 18% for 2026,” while reiterating an expense aim of “annual positive operating leverage.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: asked whether long-term profitability targets on Slide 15 might change under Seiler’s leadership; President & COO Seiler responded, “every quarter or more often, we look at all of these metrics,” and “at this point, we still think these are good metrics for us to be working on,” while noting the efficiency ratio “blipped up a little bit this quarter” and is expected “to kind of return.”
  • Tamayo, Raymond James: asked how to model seasonality and the margin path after late-quarter loan growth; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann said a Q1 day-count impact is typical but “it was more of a timing difference,” and Seiler confirmed margin expectations: “the margin comes back up into the range in the second quarter and then relatively stable from there? David Seiler: Yes.”
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: pressed on whether Q1 expenses are an appropriate run rate; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann agreed, “Exactly. Yes, spot on,” aligning to a high-single-digit annualized view implied by the question.
  • Rulis, D.A. Davidson: asked for the mix of loan growth between new vs. existing clients; President & COO Seiler said growth “was spread fairly evenly,” and CEO & Director Chambas added, “always our growth is going to be driven by new.”
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: asked about fee-income momentum and SBA; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann reiterated “10% year-over-year growth expectations,” and Seiler said SBA was expected “to be a little bit higher this quarter” but pipelines suggest it will be “relatively flat going forward.”
  • Race, Piper Sandler: asked about Q2 growth magnitude; Seiler responded, “I think that's probably reasonable for Q2,” citing payoffs “in flux,” while reiterating “we still expect to be at 10% for the year.”
  • Race, Piper Sandler: asked about charge-offs and workout timing; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann said charge-offs were “about 25 basis points” in Q1 versus “around 20 basis points on average for the year,” while Seiler said on the CRE NPA, “we are pursuing foreclosure,” with timing “more the back half of the year,” and on the ABL credit, “an end of the year type event most likely.”
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: asked about fees-in-lieu and provisioning; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann said fees-in-lieu were “about $2.2 million,” and on provisioning, “provisioning for growth off this reserve level with the 20 basis points, I think that's appropriate,” adding the macro outlook is “the wildcard.”
  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: asked where loan growth is most likely and whether hiring continues; Seiler pointed to “ABL,” “accounts receivable finance,” “Kansas City,” and “Southern Wisconsin markets,” and said another Kansas City hire is possible: “we could have another add.”
  • Martin, Brean Capital: asked about fee-income lumpiness; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann said “there's still going to be lumpiness,” citing “swap fees and SBA gains,” while describing efforts to increase stability through Private Wealth, service charges, and increased SBIC fund deployment within a “5% limit.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated congratulations and a focus on potential pressure points (expense run-rate, Q2 growth deceleration, margin modeling, provisioning, and credit timelines), including Rulis stating expenses “seemed a little high.”
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly using expectation-setting around normalization and variability, including Seiler’s “pipelines are lighter going into Q2” and Spielmann’s “there's still going to be lumpiness.”
  • Versus last quarter, the dialogue shifted from dissecting a new nonaccrual downgrade and its NIM impact to execution on growth and deposit gathering, while still returning to credit-resolution timelines and provisioning assumptions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most material new development was leadership: Q1 included Seiler’s statement that it was “Corey's last call before his retirement next week,” with Chambas staying on the Board, whereas Q4 did not include a CEO transition timeline.
  • Guidance language tightened around near-term growth pacing: Q4 emphasized pipelines “look strong for the first quarter” and an expectation that growth would “rebound,” while Q1 stressed Q2 moderation due to “pull-forward” growth and “known payoffs,” followed by “normalization in the second half of the year.”
  • Credit discussion evolved from identifying and contextualizing the $20.4 million CRE downgrade in Q4 to reporting Q1 actions, including a “$3.4 million” land loan sale “at par,” and setting expectations that further progress is more likely “before the second half of 2026.”
  • Margin framing moved from Q4’s reported compression tied to the nonaccrual interest reversal to Q1’s emphasis on timing noise and returning toward the “3.60% to 3.65%” target range.

Risks and Concerns

  • Seiler cited “the same wildcard as everyone else” and said management will “continue to monitor for any impact of oil prices and geopolitical uncertainty.” (President & COO Seiler)
  • Management highlighted competitive pressures while emphasizing discipline: Spielmann noted a “competitive environment” for deposit pricing and described using wholesale funding “to match-fund fixed rate loans and maintain NIM stability.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • Credit resolution timing remains uncertain: Seiler said the remaining CRE nonperforming portfolio resolution timing “will be variable,” with foreclosure proceedings implying limited progress before the second half of 2026, and the ABL case moving “very, very slowly” through court. (President & COO Seiler)

Final Takeaway

Corey Chambas described Q1 as a “strong start to 2026,” led by outsized loan growth and double-digit core deposit growth, while David Seiler cautioned that Q2 pipelines are “lighter” with “known payoffs” after growth was pulled into March. Brian Spielmann maintained the company’s annual framework of a 3.60% to 3.65% net interest margin and 10% fee-income growth, while acknowledging quarterly noise, expense seasonality, and continued lumpiness in certain fee lines. Management also highlighted measurable progress on the previously downgraded CRE relationship through a $3.4 million sale at par, but guided that additional resolution is likely weighted to the back half of 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Rexford outlines $400M-$500M dispositions as it raises 2026 core FFO per share midpoint by $0.02
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The Rexford team delivered a strong quarter" and "set a record for leasing activity, executing 4.1 million square feet of leases," CEO & Director Laura Clark said, adding that "the decisive actions we are taking to advance our strategic priorities are driving top- and bottom-line growth" (CEO & Director Laura Clark).
  • Clark said the company has "closed on $144 million of dispositions with another $170 million under contract or accepted offer," framing the plan as "derisking cash flows" and "reducing development exposure" while supporting "accretive capital recycling" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Clark emphasized that "share repurchases remain a compelling driver of FFO and NAV per share accretion," and said, "In the first quarter, we executed $200 million of share repurchases" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Clark pointed to tenant-demand indicators, including that "current leasing interest on our vacant spaces increased to approximately 90% compared to 75% last quarter and a year ago," while adding "market fundamentals are still under pressure" in certain areas (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Chief Operating Officer John Nahas said first-quarter leasing was "comprised of 144 deals averaging 29,000 square feet with approximately 70% coming from renewals" and reported "cash re-leasing spreads for the quarter were negative 15.4%... and negative 1.8%, excluding the Tireco renewal" (Chief Operating Officer John Nahas).
  • CFO Michael Fitzmaurice said Q1 "Core FFO per share of $0.61" was "up $0.02 sequentially" and added, "We ended the quarter with net debt to adjusted EBITDA of 4.5x and $1.3 billion of total liquidity with no significant maturities until 2027" (Chief Financial Officer Michael Fitzmaurice).

Outlook

  • Fitzmaurice said, "We are raising our full year Core FFO per share midpoint by $0.02" and also "raised our same-property NOI growth outlook by 50 basis points at the midpoint" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • He said average same-property occupancy is now expected to be "95.1% to 95.6%, up 30 basis points at the midpoint," while the "bad debt assumption of 75 basis points of revenue remains unchanged" and "net effective re-leasing spreads of 5% to 10%" also remain unchanged (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • Fitzmaurice kept other guideposts intact, including "G&A of approximately $60 million" and "interest expense of approximately $112 million" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • On value creation, he said the company expects to "stabilize and commence rent on approximately 1.1 million square feet of value-added projects, generating $17 million of annualized NOI" and reiterated "approximately $12 million of annualized in-place NOI will come offline related to 2026 construction starts" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).

Financial Results

  • Fitzmaurice attributed Q1 performance to "stronger NOI growth and accretive share buybacks" and said sequential improvement was driven "primarily by lower G&A" along with buybacks and NOI (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • He reported same-property NOI growth was "90 basis points on a net effective basis and negative 40 basis points on cash," and said "bad debt... was elevated this quarter" but "concentrated in a few tenants and not broad-based" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • Fitzmaurice said buybacks were funded by dispositions: "Disposition proceeds were redeployed into share buybacks" and "we bought back $200 million of shares at a weighted average price of $36" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).

Q&A

  • Craig Mailman, Citi: asked where leasing strength is showing up; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said demand themes included "construction-related uses, advanced manufacturing... food and beverage" and added that for Class A, "there are exceptions" including "South Bay" and pockets of "San Diego" and "Long Beach" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Samir Khanal, BofA: asked to reconcile improving activity vs. slower development leasing; CEO & Director Clark said "we are encouraged by the early signs of improvement" but "market fundamentals are under pressure" with "net absorption... negative" and "vacancy ticked up" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Greg McGinniss, Scotiabank: asked who is buying dispositions and cap rates; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said user sales produced "pretty strong cap rates" and "on a blended basis, we were below 4% this quarter" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Michael Griffin, Evercore: asked where market rents are and spread guidance context; CFO Fitzmaurice said re-leasing expectations are "between 5% and 10%" net effective and "flat to negative 5%" cash, adding "we do expect re-leasing spreads to reaccelerate through the back half of this year" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • Michael Mueller, JPMorgan: asked if buybacks require more dispositions; CFO Fitzmaurice said, "buybacks are tied to disposition activity" and reiterated disposition expectations "between $400 million and $500 million" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • John Kim, BMO: asked whether buybacks could be paused if the stock reaction stays muted; CEO & Director Clark said the company will "continue to assess" capital allocation for "the highest risk-adjusted returns" and pointed again to the "disconnect" between intrinsic value and trading price (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Vince Tibone, Green Street: asked if record leasing is driven by renewals and whether Rexford is pushing early renewals; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said "we're prioritizing" renewals as part of "our overall strategy to prioritize occupancy" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho: asked for leasing/commencement square footage targets and whether Rexford would consider a much larger portfolio sale; CFO Fitzmaurice said guidance assumes rent commencement of "between 8 million and 8.5 million this year," and CEO & Director Clark said dispositions remain focused on recycling "on an accretive basis" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice; CEO & Director Clark).
  • Richard Anderson, Cantor: asked about demand linkage from advanced manufacturing/data centers; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said "data centers is not really a core component" but for advanced manufacturing "the answer is yes... a very bold, connected line" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Nicholas Thillman, Baird: asked about shorter lease terms; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said Rexford may "proactively try to shorten terms strategically" depending on leverage and that smaller units "tend to have shorter terms anyway" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Brendan Lynch, Barclays: asked the long-term plan for the Tireco asset; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said, "it's not really a read-through to any longer-term strategic plan for that asset" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Young Ku, Wells Fargo: asked whether pro forma redevelopment rents imply embedded growth; CFO Fitzmaurice said, "No, that has to do with the mix issue" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical around the durability of improving demand and the payoff from buybacks, including questions that "reconcile" improving fundamentals with "still taking a bit longer" on development leasing and whether buybacks would be "pausing" if the dynamic continues (Samir Khanal, BofA; John Kim, BMO).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly pairing encouragement with caveats such as "we are encouraged by the early positive signs" while also stating "market fundamentals are still under pressure" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Versus last quarter’s repeated framing that the company was "not yet able to call an inflection point," this quarter management emphasized "incremental improvement" and pointed to leading indicators like "leasing interest" rising to "approximately 90%" (CEO & Director Clark).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 highlighted execution and raised guidance, with Fitzmaurice saying the company is "raising" the Core FFO midpoint by "$0.02," versus Q4 introducing 2026 guidance of "$2.35 to $2.40" and emphasizing "near-term pressure impacting our 2026 growth expectations" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice; CEO & Director Laura Clark).
  • Disposition and capital-recycling themes continued, but Q1 added a larger disclosed quarter buyback pace ("$200 million") and a clearer linkage that "Disposition proceeds were redeployed into share buybacks," while Q4 framed repurchases as a consideration "subject to" discount and capital needs (Chief Financial Officer Michael Fitzmaurice).
  • Analysts remained focused across both calls on Tireco, leasing spreads, occupancy, and the buyer pool for dispositions; in Q1, questions broadened to whether the market is "bottoming" and whether buybacks are being "rewarded" (Greg McGinniss, Scotiabank; John Kim, BMO).

Risks and Concerns

  • Clark said "market fundamentals are still under pressure" and tied stabilization to needing "net absorption turning positive... vacancy moving down and rates firming" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Nahas reported the market context remained challenging: "negative net absorption" and "rents declining approximately 70 basis points compared to last quarter," while noting Class A demand in some submarkets remains slow and has led to "delayed rent commencement" on delivered projects (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Fitzmaurice acknowledged "near-term pressure from re-leasing spreads" driven by "market rent decline over the past 3 years" and positioned mitigation around occupancy focus, the value-creation pipeline, and capital recycling (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a record leasing quarter alongside an active capital-recycling program, including $144 million of closed dispositions, $170 million under contract or accepted offer, and $200 million of buybacks. The company raised full-year Core FFO midpoint guidance by $0.02 and increased same-property NOI growth outlook, while continuing to flag pressure from negative net absorption, soft pockets in demand (notably some Class A segments), elevated but concentrated bad debt, and near-term re-leasing spread headwinds that management expects to improve in the back half of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: J&J's Xarelto, Invokana to become available through TrumpRx
data.description:
• Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) will make its blood thinner Xarelto (rivaroxaban) and type 2 diabetes medicine Invokana (canagliflozin) available for purchase through the TrumpRx website. 
  • The drug giant will also offer the diabetes drugs metformin and metformin extended-release, per a CBS News report.
  • J&J, like other pharmaceutical companies, previously agreed to work with the administration to provide some of its drugs discounted and build new plants in exchange for not having its imported drugs subject to tariffs.
  • In February, the company said it would spend $1B to build a cell therapy manufacturing facility in Pennsylvania.
